narrative: I'm sorry to share that the law school's admissions model predicted a 66% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to your application's rejection—they use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. Your profile shows strong undergraduate achievements, like an LSAT score of 45 well above the typical 37 and a GPA of 3.4 exceeding the average 3.2, alongside upper-middle family income, being a white female planning full-time studies. However, your class rankings paint a different picture: you ranked in the bottom decile (10th) in both your first and third years of school, far below the middle-of-the-pack averages around 5.5-5.7. The model's decision hinged most heavily on these school performance rankings, with your third-year rank having the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure, followed closely by your first-year rank. Your family income level came next in influence, also nudging the prediction toward failure, then your LSAT score, racial background, undergraduate GPA, and gender, all contributing in that order to the risk assessment. Interestingly, your choice of full-time enrollment was the one factor that slightly favored a passing prediction, though its impact was minimal compared to the others. Overall, while your test scores and GPA suggest real potential, the model weighed your consistently low class rankings as the biggest red flags, seeing them as stronger signals of bar exam struggles than your other strengths. Compared to typical students, your deciles stand out as unusually low, which amplified their negative sway here. Looking ahead, you can't change past school rankings, family background, gender, race, or even your solid LSAT and GPA now, but focusing on full-time dedication could help, as it mildly supports success. To boost your bar exam odds realistically, prioritize rigorous law school study habits from day one to climb higher in class deciles—perhaps through targeted tutoring or time management, since the model flags early and later-year performance as key. Retaking the LSAT for an even higher score might also tip future models in your favor, given its notable role. With your strong baseline stats, dedicated prep could turn this around; many overcome similar predictions through focused effort. Keep pursuing your goal—you've got solid foundations to build on.
